subtopic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
title: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
summary: At distance of closest approach, the kinetic energy of the particle will convert completely into electrostatic potential energy.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- At distance of closest approach, the kinetic energy of the particle will convert completely into electrostatic potential energy. Here, $$ K.E=\frac{1}{2}m v^{2} $$ Electrostatic potential energy, $$ \begin{aligned}U&=\frac{K Q q}{d}\\ &=K\frac{(2e)(Z e)}{d}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=\mathbf{K}\frac{(2e)(\mathbf{Z}e)}{d} $$ $$ d\propto\frac{1}{m} $$
- The energy of projectile is directly proportional to $ Z_{1}Z_{2} $.
- From question $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{(2e)(Z e)}{r_{0}} $$ $$ r_{0}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{2Z e^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}} $$ $$ r_{0}\propto\frac{1}{m} $$

subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
title: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
summary: According to the question, Electron Electron passes from fifth energy level to ground level means, $$…
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Here, $ r_1 = 5.3 \times 10^{-11} $ m Radius of third orbit, $ r_3 = n^2 r_1 $ $ r_3 = (3)^2 \times 5.3 \times 10^{-11} $ m (As, n = 3) $ r_3 = 47.7 \times 10^{-11} $ m $ r_3 = 4.77 \times 10^{-10} $ m
- For first excited state, $ n_{1}=2 $ For second excited state, $ n_{2}=3 $ $$ \frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\frac{n_{2}^{2}}{n_{1}^{2}}=\frac{3^{2}}{2^{2}}=\frac{9}{4} $$
- The energy of an electron in Bohr's orbit of hydrogen atom is given by the expression, $$ \mathrm{E}_{n}=-\frac{2\pi^{2}m e^{4}Z^{2}}{n^{2}h^{2}(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}=-13.6\frac{Z^{2}}{n^{2}}\mathbf{e}\mathbf{V} $$ Since, Z = 1 for hydrogen atom. So, the above equation further simplifies to, $$ \mathrm{E}_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}}\mathrm{~e V} $$
- Bohr's model is not valid for singly ionised neon atom $ \mathrm{(Ne^{+})} $.
- In Bohr's model of H-atom, $$ \mathbf{K.E}=|\mathbf{T E}|=\frac{|\mathbf{U}|}{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{K.E=3.4~e V} $$ $$ \mathrm{U}=-6.8\;{\mathrm{e V}} $$
- $$ \begin{array}{c}{m_{\upmu}=207~m_{\mathrm{e}}}\\ {r=0.51~\mathring{\mathrm{A}}}\end{array} $$ $$ r_{n}\propto\frac{1}{m} $$ $$ r_{1}=\frac{0.51\times10^{-10}}{207}=2.56\times10^{-13}m $$ Since $$ \mathrm{E}\propto m_{e} $$ $$ \circ\circ $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{1}=-13.6\times207=-2.8\mathrm{k e V} $$
- We have that, KE = -(total energy) So, kinetic energy: total energy = 1 : - 1
- Time period of an electron in Bohr orbit is, then $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{T}&=\frac{4\varepsilon_{0}^{2}h^{3}n^{3}}{m e^{4}}\\\mathrm{T}&\propto n^{3}\\\frac{\mathrm{T}_{1}}{\mathrm{T}_{2}}&=\frac{n_{1}^{3}}{n_{2}^{3}}\\\mathrm{T}_{1}&=8\mathrm{T}_{2}\\\left(\frac{8\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{T}_{2}}\right)&=\frac{n_{1}^{3}}{n_{2}^{3}}\\\left(\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}\right)^{3}&=(2^{3})\\\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}&=2\\n_{1}&=2n_{2}\end{aligned} $$
- According to the question, Electron Electron passes from fifth energy level to ground level means, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}_{5}-\mathrm{E}_{1}&=\frac{hc}{\lambda}\\\Longrightarrow\quad\frac{\mathrm{Rh}c}{25}-\mathrm{Rh}c&=\frac{hc}{\lambda}\\\Longrightarrow\quad\frac{24}{25}\mathrm{R}&=\frac{1}{\lambda}\end{aligned} $$ But $$ p=\frac{h}{\lambda} $$ and $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{\boldsymbol{h}}{m\lambda}=\frac{24}{25}\frac{\mathbf{R}\boldsymbol{h}}{m} $$
- We know that, $ E = \frac{-13.6}{n^{2}} Z^{2} $ for first excited state n = 2 and for $ \mathrm{He}^{+} $, Z = 2 $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{-13.6}{2^{2}}\times2^{2}=-13.6\mathrm{eV} $$
- For n = 1, $ \mathrm{E}_{1} = -\frac{13.6}{(1)^{2}} = -13.6\,\mathrm{eV} $ For n = 3, $$ \mathrm{E}_{3}=-\frac{13.6}{\left(3\right)^{2}}=-1.51\mathrm{eV} $$ So, required energy $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=\mathrm{E}_{3}-\mathrm{E}_{1}\\&=-\left(1.51\right)-\left(-13.6\right)=12.09\mathrm{eV}\end{aligned} $$
- We know that, Number of spectral lines, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{N}&=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\\\frac{n(n-1)}{2}&=6\\\Rightarrow n^{2}-n-12&=0\\\Rightarrow(n-4)(n+3)&=0\\\Rightarrow n&=4\end{aligned} $$
- Ground state energy of hydrogen atom $$ E_{1}=-13.6eV $$ Energy of electron in first excited state $ (n=2) $ $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}=-\frac{13.6}{\left(2\right)^{2}}\mathrm{eV} $$ ∴ Excitation energy, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta E&=E_{2}-E_{1}\\&=-\frac{13.6}{4}-(-13.6)\\&=-3.4+13.6=10.2eV\end{aligned} $$
- Energy of $ n^{\text{th}} $ orbit of hydrogen atom is given by, $$ \mathrm{E}_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}}\mathrm{eV} $$ for ground state, n = 1 $$ \mathrm{E}_{1}=-\frac{13.6}{1^{2}}=-13.6\mathrm{eV} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}=-\frac{13.6}{2^{2}}=-3.4\,\mathrm{eV} $$ $$ \mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}=|\mathbf{E}_{2}|=3.4~e\mathbf{V} $$

title: Objective I
summary: **Characteristic X-rays**: the incident electron ejects an inner-shell (e.g., $K$-shell) electron.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Bohr's **first postulate**: electrons revolve around the nucleus in **specific (quantised) circular orbits** called **stationary states** or **allowed orbits**. In these orbits, they do **not radiate energy** despite the centripetal acceleration (this contradicts classical electrodynamics but is adopted as a postulate). Answer: (B).
- Bohr's **second postulate** (quantisation of angular momentum): $$L = mvr = n\hbar = \frac{nh}{2\pi}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ where $n$ is the principal quantum number and $\hbar = h/(2\pi)$. This is the condition that selects the allowed orbits. Answer: (B).
- From Bohr's model: $$r_n = n^2 a_0 \quad \text{where } a_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \approx 0.529\,\text{Å}$$ The radius of the first orbit ($n=1$) is the **Bohr radius** $a_0$. The radius scales as $n^2$: $r_1 : r_2 : r_3 = 1 : 4 : 9$. Answer: (B).
- $$E_n = -\frac{13.6}{n^2}\,\rm eV$$ The negative sign means the electron is bound. Ground state ($n=1$): $E_1 = -13.6\,\rm eV$. The ionisation energy is $13.6\,\rm eV$. Energy increases (becomes less negative) with $n$: the electron is more loosely bound in higher orbits. Answer: (B).
- The **Rydberg formula**: $$\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H\!\left(\frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2}\right), \quad n_i > n_f$$ where $R_H \approx 1.097\times10^7\,\rm m^{-1}$ is the Rydberg constant. For emission, $n_i > n_f$ and the bracket is positive, giving a real (positive) wavelength. Spectral series: Lyman ($n_f=1$, UV), Balmer ($n_f=2$, visible), Paschen ($n_f=3$, IR), etc. Answer: (A).
- The Balmer series: transitions to $n_f = 2$ from $n_i = 3, 4, 5, \ldots$ The first line (H$\alpha$, $n=3\to2$): $\lambda = 656\,\rm nm$ (red). The series limit ($n\to\infty\to2$): $\lambda = 365\,\rm nm$ (near-UV). The Balmer series spans the **visible** range (~400–700 nm) and was the first to be discovered because it falls in the visible. Answer: (B).
- X-rays are produced in an **X-ray tube**: electrons are accelerated through a high voltage and strike a heavy metal target (anode, usually tungsten). Two processes produce X-rays: 1. **Bremsstrahlung** (braking radiation): electrons decelerate rapidly → emit X-ray photons with a continuous spectrum. 2. **Characteristic X-rays**: electrons knock out inner-shell electrons; outer electrons fill the vacancies, emitting photons with specific energies characteristic of the target material. Answer: (B).
- The most energetic X-ray photon is produced when an electron converts all its kinetic energy $eV$ into one photon: $$h\nu_{\rm max} = eV \implies \lambda_{\rm min} = \frac{c}{\nu_{\rm max}} = \frac{hc}{eV}$$ This is the **Duane–Hunt law**. The short-wavelength cutoff $\lambda_{\rm min}$ is independent of the target material and decreases as the tube voltage increases. Answer: (A).
- **Characteristic X-rays**: the incident electron ejects an inner-shell (e.g., $K$-shell) electron. This creates a vacancy. An outer-shell electron (e.g., $L$ or $M$) drops into the vacancy, releasing a photon with energy equal to the difference in binding energies: $$h\nu = E_K - E_L \quad (K_\alpha \text{ line})$$ The energies are characteristic of the target element → used for elemental identification. **Bremsstrahlung** (option A) produces the continuous spectrum, not characteristic lines. Answer: (C).
- Crystal planes act as diffraction gratings for X-rays because the interplanar spacing ($\sim 10^{-10}$m) is comparable to X-ray wavelengths. **Bragg's law**: $$2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$$ where $d$ is the interplanar spacing, $\theta$ is the glancing angle, and $n$ is the order. X-ray crystallography determined the structure of DNA (Watson & Crick, using Franklin's data) and countless proteins. Answer: (B).

formulas.latex: r_n = n^2 a_0 \quad \text{where } a_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \approx 0.529\,\text{Å}

title: Objective II
summary: - **A** ✓: Characteristic X-rays appear as sharp lines at specific wavelengths determined by the target element.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- All four are Bohr's postulates: - **A** ✓: Electrons in circular orbits (Bohr's first postulate). - **B** ✓: No radiation in stationary orbits (Bohr's first postulate). - **C** ✓: $L = n\hbar$ (Bohr's second postulate — quantisation). - **D** ✓: $E = h\nu = E_i - E_f$ (Bohr's third postulate — radiation condition). Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).
- - **Lyman** ($n_f=1$): UV region (91.2–122 nm). ✓ - **Balmer** ($n_f=2$): Visible region (365–656 nm). ✗ - **Paschen** ($n_f=3$): Near infrared (820 nm – 1875 nm). ✗ - **Brackett** ($n_f=4$): Infrared. ✗ Only the Lyman series is entirely in the UV. Answer: (A).
- - **A** ✓: Bohr's model works only for hydrogen-like (single-electron) atoms. Multi-electron atoms have electron–electron interactions not accounted for. - **B** ✓: Fine structure (due to spin-orbit coupling) requires relativistic quantum mechanics (Dirac equation). - **C** ✓: Bohr assumes precise circular orbits with definite radius and momentum — violating Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. - **D** ✗: Bohr's model gives the correct energy levels for hydrogen: $E_n = -13.6/n^2\,\rm eV$. This is not a limitation. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- - **A** ✓: X-rays are EM waves (wavelength 0.01–10 nm), discovered by Röntgen (1895). - **B** ✓: X-ray photons have high energy ($\sim$keV) → can ionise gas molecules → used in Geiger counters and ionisation chambers. - **C** ✗: As electromagnetic waves, X-rays are **not** deflected by electric or magnetic fields (unlike electrons or other charged particles). (The Compton effect involves scattering by electrons, not deflection of the wave itself by fields.) - **D** ✓: X-rays cause fluorescence in materials like zinc sulfide, calcium tungstate — used in X-ray imaging screens. Answer: (A), (B), (D).
- - **A** ✓: Characteristic X-rays appear as sharp lines at specific wavelengths determined by the target element. Bremsstrahlung is continuous from $\lambda_{\rm min}$ to infinity. - **B** ✓: Characteristic line energies are specific to the target ($K_\alpha$, $K_\beta$, etc. depend on energy levels of that element). Bremsstrahlung depends only on electron energy, not the target material. - **C** ✓: The short-wavelength cutoff ($\lambda_{\rm min} = hc/eV$) applies to Bremsstrahlung. Characteristic X-rays appear at fixed wavelengths that may or may not coincide with $\lambda_{\rm min}$. - **D** ✗: The maximum photon energy in Bremsstrahlung is $eV$ (the full electron KE). Characteristic X-rays have energies equal to inner-shell energy differences, which can be less than $eV$ (they require the electron to have at least enough energy to eject the inner-shell electron, but the emitted photon energy is fixed by atomic levels). Answer: (A), (B), (C).

title: Overview
summary: Bohr used the concept of quantization of angular momentum.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Bohr used the concept of quantization of angular momentum. For stationary orbits, Angular momentum, $$ L=\frac{nh}{2\pi} $$ where, n = 1, 2, 3, ..., etc. 23.(D) $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}\Rightarrow\lambda=\frac{h c}{\mathrm{E}}\Rightarrow\lambda\propto\frac{1}{\mathrm{E}} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{1} $ $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{1}=-\left(\frac{5\mathrm{E}}{2}\right)+4\mathrm{E}=\frac{3\mathrm{E}}{2} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{2} $, $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{2}=-2\mathrm{E}-(-3\mathrm{E}) $$ $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{2}=-2\mathrm{E}+3\mathrm{E}=

subtopic: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
title: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}\Rightarrow\lambda=\frac{h c}{\mathrm{E}}\Rightarrow\lambda\propto\frac{1}{\mathrm{E}} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{1} $ $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{1}=-\left(\frac{5\mathrm{E}}{2}\right)+4\mathrm{E}=\frac{3\mathrm{E}}{2} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{2} $, $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{2}=-2\mathrm{E}-(-3\mathrm{E}) $$ $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{2}=-2\mathrm{E}+3\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{3} $, $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{3}=-2\mathrm{E}-(-4\mathrm{E}) $$ $$ =2\mathrm{E} $$ So. $$ \lambda_{2}=2\lambda_{3} $$ $$ \lambda_{2}>\lambda_{1} $$ - The spectral series given in the question shows the electronic transitions from higher energy levels ( $ n_{2}=5,6,\ldots $) to the level ( $ n_{1}=4 $), this series is the Brackett series. - Shortest wavelength in Balmer series is $ \lambda $. For Balmer series, $ n_{1}=2 $, $ n_{2}=\infty $ $$ \begin{aligned}We know,\frac{1}{\lambda}&=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)\\\frac{1}{\lambda}&=\frac{R}{2^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ For Bracket series, $ n_{1}=4 $, $ n_{2}=\infty $ So, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}&=\mathbf{R}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)\\\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}&=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{4^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ Divide eq (i) by (ii), we get we get $$ \frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=\frac{1\times4^{2}}{2^{2}}=4 $$ $$ \lambda^{\prime}=4\lambda $$ #### ! Caution Students are often confused in finding the correct option. For it just find out the relation of the physical quantity and calculate the answer. - Using Rydberg formula, we have, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{\lambda}&=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)\\&=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right)=\frac{5R}{36}\end{aligned} $$ and $$ \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}=R\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)=\frac{7R}{144} $$ On dividing eqns. (i) by (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}&=\frac{5R}{36}\times\frac{144}{7R}=\frac{20}{7}\\\lambda^{\prime}&=\frac{20}{7}\lambda\end{aligned} $$ - Infrared radiation found in Paschen, Bracket and pfund series and it is obtain when electron transitions from high energy level to minimum $3^{rd}$ energy level. - We know that, $$ \begin{array}{c}n=4\\\downarrow\quad\mathrm{E}_{1}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{1}}\\\downarrow\quad\mathrm{E}_{2}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{2}}\\\downarrow\quad n=2\end{array} $$ Energy $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}\right] $$ According to the question, $$ \mathrm{E}_{1}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{1}}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right] $$ And $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{2}}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right] $$ On dividing eq. (ii) by (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}&=\frac{\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{16}}=\frac{20}{7}\\&=20:7\end{aligned} $$ - According to the question, Energy released = (-3.4) - (-13.6) = 10.2 eV Using Einstein's photoelectric equation $$ \mathbf{W}=\mathbf{E}-\mathbf{e}\mathbf{V}=h\boldsymbol{v} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\nu&=\frac{\mathrm{E}-e\mathrm{V}}{h}=\frac{(10.2-3.57)e}{6.67\times10^{-34}}\\\nu&=\frac{6.03\times1.6\times10^{-19}}{6.67\times10^{-34}}\\&=1.6\times10^{15}\mathrm{Hz}\end{aligned} $$ - Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{W}+\mathrm{KE}_{\text{max}}\\ =\mathrm{W}+\mathrm{eV}_{0}\\ =2.75+10\\ =12.75\,\mathrm{eV}\end{array} $$
∴ Difference of $n=4$ and $n=1$ Energy level is 12.75 eV Here, higher energy level is $n=4$ and lower energy level is $n=1$.
##### Mnemonics Order of Spectral lines in a Hydrogen Atom Lion Bechara Pyasa hai Bakri ko Paani Free Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, Brackett, Pfund. -
n=513 eV0.58 eV
n=412.09 eV0.65 eV
n=310.2 eV1.51 eV
n=213.6 eV3.4 eV
n=113.6 eV1.9 eV
But 11.1 eV is not present in the diagram. So, 11.1 eV is not possible. - We know that, Lyman series for H-ion, $$ \frac{hc}{\lambda}=\operatorname{Rhc}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right) $$ and for H-like atom. $$ \frac{hc}{\lambda}=Z^{2}\mathrm{Rh}c\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right) $$ compare we get, $$ \left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right)=Z^{2}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right) $$ Solving we get, Z = 2 - According to question, $$ \begin{aligned}-13.6+12.1&=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}}\\\Rightarrow\quad n^{2}&=\frac{13.6}{1.5}=9\\\Rightarrow\quad n&=3\end{aligned} $$ Number of spectral lines emitted, $$ \begin{aligned}&lines emitted,\\ &=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=\frac{3\times2}{2}\\ &=3\\ \end{aligned} $$ -
$$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda} $$ $$ E_{CA}=E_{CB}+E_{BA} $$ $$ \frac{hc}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{hc}{\lambda_{2}} $$ $$ \frac{1}{\lambda_{3}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}} $$ $$ \lambda_{3}=\frac{\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}+\lambda_{2}} $$ - Energy of photon, $ \Delta E = E_{2} - E_{1} $ $$ \begin{aligned}&=-\frac{13.6}{9}-\left(\frac{-13.6}{4}\right)\\&=13.6\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right]\\&=1.9eV\end{aligned} $$ - Jump to second orbit leads to Balmer series. When an electron jumps from $4^{\mathrm{th}}$ orbit to $2^{\mathrm{nd}}$, we get second line of Balmer series.

title: reNEET2026
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Rutherford scattering gives $N(\theta)\propto\dfrac{1}{\sin^4(\theta/2)}$, so $N$ decreases sharply with increasing $\theta$.
- $\dfrac{ke^2}{r}=mv^2\Rightarrow r=\dfrac{ke^2}{mv^2}=\dfrac{9\times10^9(1.6\times10^{-19})^2}{9\times10^{-31}\times25.6\times10^{10}}=1\times10^{-9}$ m, so $x=1.$
